perturbation.original_text: b. keep all Confidential Information strictly confidential by using a reasonable degree of care, but not less than the degree of care used by it in safeguarding its own confidential information; and
perturbation.changed_text: b. keep all Confidential Information confidential by using what they consider to be an appropriate degree of care, considering their current resources; and
perturbation.explanation: The term 'what they consider to be an appropriate degree of care, considering their current resources' introduces ambiguity and makes the standard of care subjective and dependent on the recipient's resources. This weakens the obligation to protect confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statutes § 815.04 - Criminal Penalties for Misappropriation of Trade Secrets
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute 815.04 defines criminal penalties for the misappropriation of trade secrets. By allowing the recipient to use what they 'consider to be an appropriate degree of care,' the modified clause potentially reduces the level of protection required, making it more difficult to prove criminal intent or negligence in the event of a trade secret misappropriation, as the standard of care becomes subjective and resource-dependent rather than objectively reasonable, thereby undermining the statute's intent to protect trade secrets. It introduces doubt about if there was a misappropriation when the care was dependent on resources.
perturbation.location: 2. Prohibition on Disclosure of Confidential Information.

Home > Laws > 2023 Florida Statutes > Title XLVI > Chapter 815 > Section 04 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2023 Florida Statutes (Including 2023C) Title XLVI CRIMES Chapter 815 COMPUTER-RELATED CRIMES Entire Chapter SECTION 04 Offenses against intellectual property. F.S. 815.04 815.04 Offenses against intellectual property. — (1) A person who willfully, knowingly, and without authorization introduces a computer contaminant or modifies or renders unavailable data, programs, or supporting documentation residing or existing internal or external to a computer, computer system, computer network, or electronic device commits an offense against intellectual property. (2) A person who willfully, knowingly, and without authorization destroys data, programs, or supporting documentation residing or existing internal or external to a computer, computer system, computer network, or electronic device commits an offense against intellectual property. (3) A person who willfully, knowingly, and without authorization discloses or takes data, programs, or supporting documentation that is a trade secret as defined in s. 812.081 or is confidential as provided by law residing or existing internal or external to a computer, computer system, computer network, or electronic device commits an offense against intellectual property. (4)(a) Except as otherwise provided in this subsection, an offense against intellectual property is a felony of the third degree, punishable as provided in s. 775.082 , s. 775.083 , or s. 775.084 . (b) If the offense is committed for the purpose of devising or executing any scheme or artifice to defraud or to obtain any property, the person commits a felony of the second degree, punishable as provided in s. 775.082 , s. 775.083 , or s. 775.084 . History. — s. 1, ch. 78-92; s. 1, ch. 94-100; s. 431, ch. 96-406; s. 1, ch. 2014-177; s. 4, ch. 2014-208; s. 5, ch. 2016-5; s. 20, ch. 2016-6; s. 31, ch. 2022-5.
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates a 'reasonable degree of care,' aligning with a standard legal expectation for protecting confidential information. The changed text weakens this by allowing the recipient to use 'what they consider to be an appropriate degree of care, considering their current resources.' This subjective standard contradicts the intent of Florida Statute 815.04, which addresses offenses against intellectual property, including trade secrets. The law explanation states that the change introduces doubt about misappropriation and the original reasonable standard of care. Snippet 1 indicates that unauthorized disclosure of trade secrets can lead to legal consequences. By allowing the recipient's resources to dictate the level of care, it weakens the protection and creates ambiguity, making it harder to prove a violation.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party agrees to use the same degree of protection it uses for its own trade secret information, and in no event less than reasonable efforts, to prevent and protect the Confidential Information, or any part thereof, from disclosure to any person other WhaQ Whe ReceiYiQg PaUW\’V ePSlR\eeV haYiQg a Qeed fRU diVclRVXUe iQ cRQQecWiRQ ZiWh Whe ReceiYiQg PaUW\’V aXWhRUi]ed XVe Rf Whe CRQfideQWial IQfRUPaWiRQ.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party agrees to try to use a similar degree of protection as it uses for its own confidential information to prevent disclosure of the Confidential Information, taking into account operational considerations.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'the same degree of protection it uses for its own trade secret information, and in no event less than reasonable efforts' with 'try to use a similar degree of protection as it uses for its own confidential information' weakens the obligation. Adding 'taking into account operational considerations' introduces significant ambiguity, allowing the receiving party to prioritize operational efficiency over security. This lowers the bar for protecting the disclosed confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statutes § 812.081 - Theft of Trade Secrets
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute 812.081 outlines the criminal offense of theft of trade secrets. By introducing 'taking into account operational considerations', the modified text weakens the protective measures required, potentially making it more difficult to prove the intent necessary for a conviction under this statute if the 'operational considerations' led to inadequate protection and subsequent theft or misappropriation. The statute requires intent to deprive or appropriate, and the ambiguity clouds whether that intent was present given their operational considerations.
perturbation.location: 4. Obligations of Receiving Party. b. No Disclosure.

Home > Laws > 2023 Florida Statutes > Title XLVI > Chapter 812 > Section 081 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2023 Florida Statutes (Including 2023C) Title XLVI CRIMES Chapter 812 THEFT, ROBBERY, AND RELATED CRIMES Entire Chapter SECTION 081 Theft of or trafficking in trade secrets; definitions; penalties; providing to foreign entities; restitution. F.S. 812.081 812.081 Theft of or trafficking in trade secrets; definitions; penalties; providing to foreign entities; restitution. — (1) As used in this section, the term: (a) “Endeavor” means to attempt or to try. (b) “Foreign agent” means any officer, employee, proxy, servant, delegate, or representative of a foreign government. (c) “Foreign instrumentality” means any agency, bureau, ministry, component, institution, association, or any legal, commercial, or business organization, corporation, firm, or entity that is substantially owned, controlled, sponsored, commanded, managed, or dominated by a foreign government. (d) “Obtain or use” has the same meaning as provided in s. 812.012 (3). (e) “Person” means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (f) “Trade secret” means the whole or any portion or phase of any formula, pattern, device, combination of devices, or compilation of information which is for use, or is used, in the operation of a business and which provides the business an advantage, or an opportunity to obtain an advantage, over those who do not know or use it. The term includes any scientific, technical, or commercial information, including financial information, and includes any design, process, procedure, list of suppliers, list of customers, business code, or improvement thereof, whether tangible or intangible, and regardless of whether or how it is stored, compiled, or memorialized physically, electronically, graphically, photographically, or in writing. Irrespective of novelty, invention, patentability, the state of the prior art, and the level of skill in the business, art, or field to which the subject matter pertains, a trade secret is considered to be: 1. Secret; 2. Of value; 3. For use or in use by the business; and 4. Of advantage to the business, or providing an opportunity to obtain an advantage, over
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to protect confidential information by replacing 'the same degree of protection it uses for its own trade secret information, and in no event less than reasonable efforts' with 'try to use a similar degree of protection as it uses for its own confidential information' and adding 'taking into account operational considerations'. This introduces ambiguity and allows prioritizing operational efficiency over security, potentially leading to inadequate protection. According to the Law Explanation, this makes it more difficult to prove the intent necessary for a conviction under Florida Statute 812.081 if operational considerations led to inadequate protection and subsequent theft or misappropriation of trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'trade secret' and outlines penalties for its theft, which becomes harder to enforce due to the ambiguous protection standard introduced in the modified contract.

perturbation.original_text: The Federal and state courts located in Duval County, Florida shall have sole and exclusive jurisdiction over any disputes arising under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Federal and state courts located in Duval County, Florida may have jurisdiction over disputes arising under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall have sole and exclusive jurisdiction' with 'may have jurisdiction' introduces ambiguity regarding the proper venue for dispute resolution. This contradicts established principles of contract law, where jurisdictional clauses provide certainty and predictability.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute 47.011 establishes the general rules for venue in Florida courts. While it doesn't explicitly mandate exclusive jurisdiction clauses in contracts, it provides a framework for determining proper venue. By making the jurisdiction permissive ('may have') rather than mandatory ('shall have sole and exclusive'), the modified clause creates uncertainty and could lead to disputes over venue, potentially conflicting with the statute's intent to provide clear guidelines for determining the proper court. This change can lead to forum shopping, increasing litigation costs and contradicting the efficiency intended by venue statutes.

Home > Laws > 2023 Florida Statutes > Title VI > Chapter 47 > Section 011 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2023 Florida Statutes (Including 2023C) Title VI CIVIL PRACTICE AND PROCEDURE Chapter 47 VENUE Entire Chapter SECTION 011 Where actions may be begun. F.S. 47.011 47.011 Where actions may be begun. — Actions shall be brought only in the county where the defendant resides, where the cause of action accrued, or where the property in litigation is located. This section shall not apply to actions against nonresidents. History. — s. 7, Nov. 21, 1829; s. 1, ch. 3721, 1887; RS 998; GS 1383; RGS 2579; CGL 4219; s. 24, ch. 57-1; s. 12, ch. 63-572; s. 6, ch. 65-1; s. 3, ch. 67-254; s. 11, ch. 73-334. Note. — Former s. 46.01.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies that Duval County, Florida courts 'shall have sole and exclusive jurisdiction,' providing a clear venue for disputes. The changed text uses 'may have jurisdiction,' introducing ambiguity. Florida Statute 47.011, as provided in Scraped Law Snippet 1, states that actions 'shall be brought only in the county where the defendant resides, where the cause of action accrued, or where the property in litigation is located.' While the statute doesn't explicitly mandate exclusive jurisdiction clauses, it establishes a framework for determining venue. By changing 'shall have sole and exclusive jurisdiction' to 'may have jurisdiction,' the contract creates uncertainty about the proper venue for disputes, potentially conflicting with the statute's intent to provide clear guidelines. This ambiguity undermines the certainty and predictability typically sought in jurisdictional clauses, therefore a contradiction exists.
